facts.text: Leonardo da Vinci spent approximately four years painting the Mona Lisa, starting around 1503.
Oil paint dries through a chemical process called oxidation, not evaporation, which is why it takes so long to dry.
The Louvre Museum in Paris is the most visited art museum in the world, attracting over 7 million visitors per year.
Impressionism got its name from Claude Monet's painting 'Impression, Sunrise' (1872), which a critic used mockingly.
Michelangelo painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling while standing, not lying on his back as popularly believed.
Charcoal is one of the oldest drawing materials, used by cave artists over 30,000 years ago.
Pablo Picasso was born on October 25, 1881, in Málaga, Spain.
The Metropolitan Museum of Art (the Met) in New York houses over 2 million works spanning 5,000 years of art.
Fresco painting involves applying pigment to wet plaster, allowing the color to become part of the wall itself.
Salvador Dalí's 'The Persistence of Memory' features melting clocks and was painted in 1931.
Banksy's identity remains unknown despite being one of the most famous artists of the 21st century.
Watercolor paints are made by suspending pigment in a water-soluble binder, usually gum arabic.
Van Gogh sold only one painting during his lifetime — 'The Red Vineyard' — for 400 francs.
The Uffizi Gallery in Florence was originally built in 1560 as offices for Florentine magistrates.
Acrylic paint was first developed in the 1940s and became commercially available in the 1950s.
Georgia O'Keeffe is known as the 'Mother of American Modernism' for her large-scale flower paintings.
Claude Monet was born on November 14, 1840, in Paris, France.
Rodin's 'The Thinker' was originally part of a larger work called 'The Gates of Hell.'
Palette knife painting creates thick, textured strokes that give a sculptural quality to canvas work.
The Mona Lisa was stolen from the Louvre on August 21, 1911, by Vincenzo Peruggia, a museum employee.
RISD (Rhode Island School of Design) is one of the oldest and most prestigious art schools in the United States.
Jackson Pollock pioneered the drip painting technique, laying canvases on the floor and pouring paint onto them.
The pigment ultramarine was historically made from ground lapis lazuli and was more expensive than gold.
Andy Warhol's Campbell's Soup Cans series, created in 1962, consists of 32 canvases.
Gouache is similar to watercolor but is opaque because it contains white pigment or chalk.
Vermeer's 'Girl with a Pearl Earring' is sometimes called the 'Mona Lisa of the North.'
The Tate Modern in London is housed in a former power station on the banks of the Thames.
Encaustic painting uses heated beeswax mixed with colored pigments and was practiced by ancient Greeks.
Rembrandt van Rijn painted over 90 self-portraits during his career, more than any other major artist.
Leonardo da Vinci was born on April 15, 1452, near the town of Vinci in Tuscany, Italy.
Pop Art emerged in the 1950s and challenged traditions by using imagery from popular culture and mass media.
Pencil graphite is graded on an H-B scale, where H means hard and B means black (soft).
The Birth of Venus by Sandro Botticelli was painted around 1485 and depicts the goddess emerging from the sea.
Plein air painting means painting outdoors, a practice championed by the Impressionists.
In 1990, thieves stole 13 works worth $500 million from the Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum in Boston.
Cubism, developed by Picasso and Braque around 1907, broke objects into geometric shapes viewed from multiple angles.
Bob Ross painted over 30,000 paintings in his lifetime and hosted 'The Joy of Painting' for 11 years.
The Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam is home to Rembrandt's famous 'The Night Watch.'
Frida Kahlo was born on July 6, 1907, in Coyoacán, Mexico City.
Pastels are pigment sticks made by combining pure pigment with a binder, producing vivid colors.
The Guggenheim Museum in New York, designed by Frank Lloyd Wright, opened in 1959.
Glazing is a technique where thin, transparent layers of paint are applied over dried layers to create depth.
Edvard Munch's 'The Scream' has four versions — two pastels and two paintings.
Abstract Expressionism emerged in New York in the 1940s and was the first major American art movement.
Beeple sold an NFT artwork for $69.3 million at Christie's in March 2021, making digital art history.
Kolinsky sable brushes, made from the tail hair of the Siberian weasel, are considered the finest painting brushes.
Michelangelo's 'David' stands 17 feet tall and was carved from a single block of Carrara marble.
The Prado Museum in Madrid houses over 8,000 paintings, including works by Velázquez, Goya, and El Greco.
Vincent van Gogh was born on March 30, 1853, in Groot-Zundert, Netherlands.
Indian ink, also called India ink, has been used for writing and drawing for over 4,000 years.
Surrealism, founded by André Breton in 1924, sought to release the creative potential of the unconscious mind.
The world's most expensive painting ever sold is Leonardo da Vinci's 'Salvator Mundi,' which fetched $450.3 million in 2017.
Procreate, a digital painting app for iPad, was released in 2011 and has become a go-to tool for digital artists.
The National Gallery in London houses over 2,300 paintings dating from the 13th to the 20th century.
Figure drawing classes use live models to help artists understand human anatomy and proportions.
Grant Wood's 'American Gothic' was painted in 1930 and features a farmer and his daughter outside a house.
Renaissance art focused on realism, perspective, and classical themes, flourishing in Italy from the 14th to 17th century.
Monet created approximately 250 oil paintings of water lilies during the last 30 years of his life.
Sotheby's, founded in 1744, is the world's oldest international auction house for art and collectibles.
Linen canvas is preferred by many oil painters because it's more durable and smoother than cotton canvas.
Salvador Dalí was born on May 11, 1904, in Figueres, Catalonia, Spain.
Street art differs from graffiti in that it typically has a message or artistic intent beyond tagging.
Georgia O'Keeffe was born on November 15, 1887, near Sun Prairie, Wisconsin.
Henri Matisse, confined to a wheelchair later in life, created art using painted paper cutouts.
The Baroque period (1600–1750) emphasized drama, movement, and emotional intensity in painting.
Adobe Photoshop, first released in 1990, revolutionized digital art and photo manipulation.
Pen and ink drawing is one of the most permanent drawing methods, favored by artists like Albrecht Dürer.
CalArts (California Institute of the Arts) was founded by Walt Disney and has produced many famous animators.
Caravaggio was known for his dramatic use of chiaroscuro — strong contrasts between light and dark.
Andy Warhol was born on August 6, 1928, in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.
Gesso is a white paint mixture applied to canvases as a primer to prevent paint from soaking into the fabric.
Picasso's 'Guernica' (1937) is an anti-war painting depicting the bombing of a Basque town during the Spanish Civil War.
The Art Institute of Chicago houses Georges Seurat's 'A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte.'
Post-Impressionists like Cézanne, Gauguin, and Van Gogh extended Impressionism while rejecting its limitations.
During World War II, the Nazis looted an estimated 600,000 paintings from museums and private collections across Europe.
Easels come in three main types: A-frame, H-frame, and single-mast, each suited to different painting styles.
The Impressionists held their first independent exhibition on April 15, 1874, in Paris.
Michelangelo was born on March 6, 1475, in Caprese, Republic of Florence.
Jackson Pollock's painting 'Number 17A' sold for approximately $200 million in 2016 in a private sale.
The Royal Academy of Arts in London, founded in 1768, holds an annual Summer Exhibition open to all artists.
Cadmium pigments produce brilliant reds, oranges, and yellows but are toxic and require careful handling.
Frida Kahlo created 143 paintings, 55 of which are self-portraits reflecting her pain and identity.
Pointillism is a technique developed by Seurat that uses tiny dots of color to create an image when viewed from a distance.
MoMA (Museum of Modern Art) in New York was founded in 1929 and holds nearly 200,000 works of modern and contemporary art.
Turpentine and mineral spirits are commonly used as solvents to thin oil paints and clean brushes.
Rembrandt van Rijn was born on July 15, 1606, in Leiden, Dutch Republic.
Bob Ross's 'The Joy of Painting' ran from 1983 to 1994 and comprised 403 episodes across 31 seasons.
The color wheel was invented by Sir Isaac Newton in 1666 when he first organized colors into a circle.
Trompe-l'oeil is a painting technique that creates the optical illusion of three-dimensional objects on a flat surface.
Banksy's 'Girl with Balloon' partially shredded itself moments after selling for $1.4 million at Sotheby's in 2018.
Egg tempera was the primary painting medium before oil paint became widespread in the 15th century.
The Hermitage Museum in St. Petersburg, Russia, has a collection of over 3 million items including paintings.
Conte crayons, made of compressed graphite or charcoal with wax, are popular for tonal drawing.
Expressionism emerged in early 20th-century Germany, prioritizing emotional experience over physical reality.
Christie's auction house was founded in 1766 and has sold some of the most expensive artworks in history.
Marc Chagall was born on July 7, 1887, in Liozna, Belarus.
A mahlstick is a padded stick painters use to steady their hand while working on fine details.
Picasso produced an estimated 50,000 works of art during his career, including paintings, drawings, and sculptures.
The Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood was founded in 1848 by young British artists who rejected industrialized art.
iPad Pro with Apple Pencil has become a serious tool for professional digital artists and illustrators.
Sfumato is a painting technique used by Leonardo da Vinci that creates soft, smoky transitions between colors.
Alexander Calder invented the mobile, a type of kinetic sculpture that moves with air currents.
The Louvre's glass pyramid, designed by I.M. Pei, was completed in 1989 and serves as the main entrance.
Vine charcoal is made from burned willow or vine sticks and is the softest, most erasable form of charcoal.
Winslow Homer is celebrated for his marine paintings depicting the power and beauty of the sea.
The Mona Lisa is surprisingly small — just 30 inches by 21 inches — and is displayed behind bulletproof glass.
Jackson Pollock was born on January 28, 1912, in Cody, Wyoming.
Hog bristle brushes are the most common type used for oil painting due to their stiffness and durability.
Art Nouveau was a decorative art movement from the 1890s to 1910s known for its organic, flowing lines.
Gustav Klimt's 'The Kiss' (1907–1908) is famous for its use of gold leaf and ornamental patterns.
The oldest known paintings are in the Chauvet Cave in France, dating back roughly 36,000 years.
Impasto is a technique where paint is applied thickly so that brush or knife marks are visible.
The Musée d'Orsay in Paris, housed in a former railway station, specializes in Impressionist and Post-Impressionist art.
Winsor & Newton, founded in 1832, is one of the world's most recognized art supply manufacturers.
Edvard Munch was born on December 12, 1863, in Ådalsbruk, Norway.
A camera obscura projects an inverted image through a small hole, and was used by Renaissance artists as a drawing aid.
Mark Rothko's color field paintings consist of large rectangular areas of color meant to evoke deep emotion.
The Starry Night was painted by Van Gogh in June 1889 while he was staying at the Saint-Paul-de-Mausole asylum.
Sable brushes hold more paint and produce smoother strokes than synthetic brushes, making them prized for watercolor.
Artemisia Gentileschi was one of the first female artists to achieve recognition in the male-dominated Baroque era.
Minimalism emerged in the 1960s, reducing art to its most essential geometric forms and colors.
Andy Warhol's studio was known as 'The Factory' and became a gathering spot for artists, musicians, and celebrities.
Sandro Botticelli was born on March 1, 1445, in Florence, Italy.
Chinese ink wash painting, also called sumi-e, uses black ink in varying dilutions to create expressive works.
The Golden Ratio (approximately 1.618) has been used by artists since antiquity to create balanced compositions.
Stretched canvas became popular in the 16th century as a lighter, cheaper alternative to wooden panels.
Henri Matisse led the Fauvist movement, which used wild, vibrant colors directly from the tube.
The Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum heist in 1990 remains the largest unsolved art theft in history.
Copic markers, made in Japan, are alcohol-based markers widely used by illustrators, manga artists, and designers.
Raphael was born on April 6, 1483, in Urbino, Italy, and died on the same date in 1520.
Alla prima (wet-on-wet) painting involves completing a work in a single session before the paint dries.
Jean-Michel Basquiat went from street artist to selling paintings for millions before his death at 27.
The Prado Museum opened to the public on November 19, 1819.
Gum turpentine, derived from pine resin, has been used as a paint solvent for centuries.
Diego Rivera was one of Mexico's most famous muralists, known for large-scale public works depicting Mexican society.
Dada was an anti-art movement born in Zurich in 1916 as a reaction to the horrors of World War I.
Vermeer is believed to have used a camera obscura to assist with his remarkably accurate compositions.
Synthetic brushes made from nylon or polyester have improved greatly and are now a popular alternative to natural hair.
The National Gallery of Art in Washington, D.C. was established by Congress in 1937 and admission is free.
Lithography, invented in 1796, is a printing process based on the principle that oil and water don't mix.
Georgia O'Keeffe's painting 'Jimson Weed/White Flower No. 1' sold for $44.4 million in 2014, a record for a female artist.
Chiaroscuro refers to the strong contrast between light and dark used to give the illusion of volume.
The Whitney Museum of American Art in New York focuses exclusively on 20th- and 21st-century American art.
Rembrandt's 'The Night Watch' was trimmed on all four sides to fit between two doors in Amsterdam's Town Hall.
Raw sienna, burnt umber, and yellow ochre are earth pigments that have been used since prehistoric times.
Andy Warhol was a leading figure in the Pop Art movement, which blurred the line between fine art and commercial art.
Henri de Toulouse-Lautrec was born on November 24, 1864, and is famous for his colorful posters of Parisian nightlife.
Yayoi Kusama's infinity rooms use mirrors and lights to create immersive, seemingly endless art installations.
Oil paint was popularized by Jan van Eyck in the early 15th century, though it was used before him.
The Art Students League of New York, founded in 1875, has taught artists including Jackson Pollock and Georgia O'Keeffe.
Etching is an intaglio printmaking technique where lines are incised into a metal plate using acid.
Paul Cézanne is often called the 'Father of Modern Art' for bridging Impressionism and Cubism.
The Sistine Chapel ceiling took Michelangelo approximately four years to complete, from 1508 to 1512.
Linseed oil is the most common drying oil used as a binder in oil paints.
Kehinde Wiley painted Barack Obama's official presidential portrait, unveiled in February 2018.
Titanium white replaced lead white as the most popular white pigment because it's non-toxic.
Frida Kahlo and Diego Rivera married on August 21, 1929, and their turbulent relationship influenced much of her art.
Art forgery has a long history — Han van Meegeren famously forged Vermeer paintings that fooled experts and even the Nazis.
Cézanne was born on January 19, 1839, in Aix-en-Provence, France.
The Bauhaus school, founded in 1919 in Weimar, Germany, combined crafts and fine arts with a focus on design.
Manga-style illustration has become a globally popular drawing form originating from Japanese comic art.
A varnish layer is applied over finished oil paintings to protect the surface and enhance color saturation.
The Venus de Milo, an ancient Greek sculpture, is displayed in the Louvre and is believed to depict Aphrodite.
Wassily Kandinsky is generally credited as the pioneer of purely abstract art, creating his first abstract work around 1910.
Turpenoid, an odorless solvent, is a popular alternative to turpentine for artists sensitive to fumes.
Mary Cassatt was an American Impressionist known for her intimate paintings of mothers and children.
The rule of thirds is a compositional guideline that divides a canvas into a 3x3 grid for balanced placement.
Leonardo da Vinci's notebooks contain over 13,000 pages of notes and drawings on art, science, and engineering.
The Rijksmuseum was founded in 1800 and reopened in 2013 after a major ten-year renovation.
Amedeo Modigliani was born on July 12, 1884, in Livorno, Italy, and is known for elongated portraits.
Stand oil is a thickened form of linseed oil that produces enamel-like, smooth paint films.
The Hudson River School was a mid-19th-century American art movement known for romantic landscape paintings.
Damien Hirst's 'The Physical Impossibility of Death in the Mind of Someone Living' features a tiger shark in formaldehyde.
Art therapy uses creative expression to help people explore emotions, reduce anxiety, and improve mental health.
Winsor & Newton's Series 7 brushes were originally made for Queen Victoria in 1866.
Giotto di Bondone is considered the first great painter of the Italian Renaissance, breaking from the Byzantine style.
Woodcut printing is one of the oldest printmaking techniques, widely used in medieval Europe and East Asia.
Henri Rousseau was a self-taught Post-Impressionist painter famous for his jungle scenes, though he never left France.
Spray paint art became popular in the 1970s and 1980s with the rise of graffiti culture in New York City.
The MoMA PS1 in Queens, New York, is one of the oldest and largest nonprofit contemporary art institutions in the US.
Van Gogh painted 'The Starry Night' from memory during the day, not while looking at the night sky.
Cochineal insects were crushed to produce the vibrant red pigment carmine, used in paintings and dyes for centuries.
Gustav Klimt was born on July 14, 1862, in Baumgarten, Austria.
Mixed media art combines two or more artistic media, such as paint with collage, fabric, or found objects.
The Frick Collection in New York occupies a mansion built in 1914 and displays Old Master paintings and European sculpture.
Ukiyo-e is a Japanese art genre featuring woodblock prints and paintings of landscapes, theater, and beauty.
Francis Bacon's 'Three Studies of Lucian Freud' sold for $142.4 million at Christie's in 2013.
Cross-hatching is a drawing technique that uses intersecting sets of parallel lines to create shading and depth.
The Albertina Museum in Vienna houses one of the largest and most important print collections in the world.
Norman Rockwell illustrated 323 covers for The Saturday Evening Post over nearly 50 years.
Retarder medium slows the drying time of acrylic paint, giving artists more time to blend and work the paint.
David Hockney's 'Portrait of an Artist (Pool with Two Figures)' sold for $90.3 million in 2018.
Graffiti art dates back to ancient Rome and Pompeii, where walls were covered with political slogans and drawings.
Silverpoint drawing uses a silver-tipped stylus on coated paper and was popular during the Renaissance.
Edward Hopper was born on July 22, 1882, and is best known for his painting 'Nighthawks' (1942).
The concept of perspective in art was formalized by Filippo Brunelleschi around 1415 in Florence.
Ai Weiwei is a Chinese contemporary artist and activist known for installations that challenge political authority.
Sable brushes are named after the sable marten, though many are actually made from other types of weasel fur.
Marcel Duchamp's 'Fountain' (1917), a porcelain urinal signed 'R. Mutt,' challenged the very definition of art.
Color theory is the study of how colors interact, combining science and art to guide painters in mixing and pairing hues.
The Centre Pompidou in Paris, designed by Renzo Piano and Richard Rogers, houses Europe's largest modern art collection.
Albrecht Dürer was born on May 21, 1471, in Nuremberg, Germany, and is considered the greatest German Renaissance artist.
Aquatint is a printmaking technique that creates tonal areas rather than lines, achieving effects similar to watercolor.
The Harlem Renaissance in the 1920s and 1930s brought African American art, music, and literature to national prominence.
Cobalt blue pigment was first synthesized in 1802 and quickly became popular for its brilliant, stable color.
J.M.W. Turner's will stipulated that his paintings be given to the British nation, resulting in the Turner Bequest of over 300 oils.
A still life painting depicts inanimate objects such as fruit, flowers, and household items.
The Phillips Collection in Washington, D.C. was the first museum of modern art in America, opening in 1921.
Winslow Homer was born on February 24, 1836, in Boston, Massachusetts.
Byzantine art flourished from the 4th to the 15th century and is characterized by golden backgrounds and religious iconography.
Poppy seed oil is sometimes used instead of linseed oil because it yellows less over time.
Louise Bourgeois created 'Maman,' a giant spider sculpture over 30 feet tall, first exhibited in 2000.
Banksy's Dismaland, a dystopian theme park art installation, opened in Weston-super-Mare, England, in August 2015.
The Venetian school of painting is known for its emphasis on color and light, led by artists like Titian and Tintoretto.
A viewfinder is a simple cardboard frame used by artists to isolate and compose a scene before painting.
Jeff Koons' 'Balloon Dog (Orange)' sold for $58.4 million in 2013, a record for a living artist at the time.
Fixative spray is applied to charcoal and pastel drawings to prevent smudging and preserve the work.
Anna Mary Robertson 'Grandma' Moses began painting at age 78 and became one of America's most celebrated folk artists.
Tempera paint dries almost immediately and has a matte finish, unlike the slow-drying gloss of oil paints.
World Art Day is celebrated annually on April 15, Leonardo da Vinci's birthday.
John Singer Sargent painted over 900 oil paintings and more than 2,000 watercolors during his prolific career.
The Musée de l'Orangerie in Paris houses eight large Water Lilies murals by Monet in two oval rooms.
Lead white was the only white pigment available to painters for thousands of years before zinc and titanium whites were developed.
Keith Haring rose to fame in the 1980s with his bold, graphic street art drawn in New York City subway stations.
Crayon comes from the French word 'craie' meaning chalk, and 'crayon' meaning pencil.
Rococo art (1720s–1780s) was characterized by ornate decoration, pastel colors, and playful, lighthearted subjects.
Auguste Rodin was born on November 12, 1840, in Paris, and is often considered the father of modern sculpture.
Dammar varnish, made from tree resin, is a traditional final varnish for oil paintings.
The Art Basel fair, held annually in Basel, Miami Beach, and Hong Kong, is one of the world's premier art events.
Berthe Morisot was the first woman to join the French Impressionists and exhibited in seven of their eight exhibitions.
Monochromatic painting uses variations of a single color to create a unified, harmonious composition.
The Tokyo National Museum, founded in 1872, is Japan's oldest and largest art museum.
Frida Kahlo's painting 'Diego y yo' sold for $34.9 million in 2021, a record for a Latin American artist.
Walnut oil was used by Leonardo da Vinci as a painting medium and dries faster than linseed oil with less yellowing.
Pablo Picasso went through several artistic periods including the Blue Period (1901–1904) and the Rose Period (1904–1906).
The oldest known hand stencils, found in caves in Indonesia, are over 40,000 years old.
Masking fluid is a liquid latex applied to watercolor paintings to preserve white areas from paint.
Pierre-Auguste Renoir was born on February 25, 1841, in Limoges, France.
Op Art (Optical Art) uses geometric patterns to create visual illusions of movement and depth.
The Barnes Foundation in Philadelphia houses one of the finest collections of Post-Impressionist and early Modern paintings.
Burnt sienna pigment gets its warm reddish-brown color from iron oxide heated at high temperatures.
In 2022, climate activists threw tomato soup at Van Gogh's 'Sunflowers' in London's National Gallery, sparking global debate.
Parchment paper was used as a drawing surface in medieval times before the widespread use of paper.
Kara Walker is known for her large-scale silhouette installations exploring race, gender, and history.
A paint palette has traditionally been made of wood, but glass and plastic palettes are now common.
The School of Athens, painted by Raphael between 1509 and 1511, adorns a wall in the Vatican's Apostolic Palace.
Damien Hirst's 'For the Love of God' is a platinum skull encrusted with 8,601 diamonds.
Soft pastels have a higher pigment-to-binder ratio than hard pastels, giving them richer color and easier blendability.
World Watercolor Month is celebrated every July to promote watercolor painting around the globe.
Diego Rivera was born on December 8, 1886, in Guanajuato, Mexico.
Egg tempera uses egg yolk as a binder, producing a luminous, fast-drying paint used since ancient Egypt.
Lucian Freud, grandson of Sigmund Freud, was known for his unflinching, thickly painted portraits.
The Mona Lisa's eyebrows are missing — either never painted or removed by past restoration attempts.
A maulstick helps steady the artist's hand by resting on the easel, allowing precise work on fine details.
The Neue Pinakothek in Munich focuses on European art of the 18th and 19th centuries.
Constructivism started in Russia around 1913 and emphasized geometric abstraction and modern materials.
Pigment is the substance that gives paint its color, derived from minerals, plants, insects, or synthetic chemicals.
Vermeer only completed about 34 paintings in his entire lifetime, working very slowly and meticulously.
Silk screening (serigraphy) was used extensively by Andy Warhol to mass-produce images of celebrities and consumer goods.
Paul Gauguin was born on June 7, 1848, in Paris, and later moved to Tahiti, where he created his most famous works.
Arches paper, made in France since 1492, is one of the most renowned watercolor papers in the world.
The Pompidou Center's exposed, color-coded piping system — blue for air, green for water, yellow for electricity — is part of its design.
An underpainting is a preliminary layer of paint that establishes values and composition before final colors are applied.
Marina Abramović's performance art piece 'The Artist Is Present' (2010) at MoMA lasted 736 hours over three months.
Cerulean blue, first synthesized in 1805, became a favorite sky color among Impressionist painters.
The Getty Center in Los Angeles, designed by Richard Meier, opened in 1997 and houses European art from the Middle Ages onward.
Caspar David Friedrich was a leading German Romantic painter known for contemplative landscapes featuring solitary figures.
Plein air painters often use pochade boxes — compact, portable easels that hold paints, brushes, and a small panel.
Gustave Courbet's 'The Origin of the World' (1866) remained hidden in private collections for over a century.
Cold-pressed watercolor paper has a slightly textured surface, making it the most versatile and popular choice.
Claes Oldenburg created oversized sculptures of everyday objects like clothespins, spoons, and typewriter erasers.
Georgia O'Keeffe moved to New Mexico in 1949 and found lasting inspiration in the desert landscape.
Dry brushing is a technique where a small amount of paint is applied with a nearly dry brush for textured effects.
Takashi Murakami's 'Superflat' style merges traditional Japanese art with anime and pop culture aesthetics.
The Vatican Museums contain roughly 70,000 works, of which about 20,000 are on display at any given time.
Sienna pigment is named after the Tuscan city of Siena, where the clay deposits were first mined.
Norman Rockwell was born on February 3, 1894, in New York City.
Action painting, a style associated with Abstract Expressionism, emphasizes the physical act of painting itself.
Fan brushes are used for blending, creating texture effects like grass, hair, and clouds.
The Venus of Willendorf, a tiny limestone figure from about 25,000 BCE, is one of the oldest known artworks.
Acrylic paint can be thinned with water but becomes water-resistant once dry, unlike watercolor.
El Greco was born on October 1, 1541, in Crete and later settled in Toledo, Spain.
Thomas Kinkade marketed himself as the 'Painter of Light' and his prints appeared in an estimated 1 in 20 American homes.
A complementary color scheme uses colors opposite each other on the color wheel, like blue and orange, for high contrast.
Museum-quality framing uses acid-free mats and UV-protective glass to preserve artwork for decades.
Hieronymus Bosch's 'The Garden of Earthly Delights' triptych is filled with surreal imagery that still puzzles scholars today.
Thinning acrylic paint with water beyond about 30% can weaken its binding properties, causing it to crack.
The Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofía in Madrid houses Picasso's 'Guernica.'
Kinetic art features movement as an integral part of the artwork, pioneered by artists like Calder and Jean Tinguely.
Cadmium yellow was first produced in the early 19th century and is prized for its bright, opaque color.
The Mona Lisa has her own mailbox at the Louvre because she receives so many letters from admirers.
Johannes Vermeer was born on October 31, 1632, in Delft, Dutch Republic.
Conceptual art, emerging in the 1960s, prioritizes ideas over the finished visual product.
Grisaille is a painting technique executed entirely in shades of gray to simulate sculpture.
Damien Hirst's spot paintings number over 1,400 but most were painted by his assistants, not by Hirst himself.
The Prix de Rome was a prestigious French art scholarship that funded study in Rome from 1663 to 1968.
Faber-Castell, founded in 1761, is one of the oldest and largest manufacturers of pencils and art supplies.
A ground is any surface preparation applied to a support (canvas, panel, paper) before painting on it.
Land art (Earth art) emerged in the 1960s, using natural landscapes as both the medium and canvas.
Toulouse-Lautrec was only 4 feet 8 inches tall due to a genetic condition that affected his bone growth.
Phthalo blue is an extremely strong, versatile synthetic pigment introduced in the 1930s.
The Artsy platform connects collectors with galleries and has listed over 1 million artworks from 4,000 galleries.
Paul Klee described drawing as 'taking a line for a walk,' emphasizing the playful nature of his art.
Scumbling is a painting technique where a thin layer of lighter, opaque paint is brushed over a dried darker layer.
Édouard Manet was born on January 23, 1832, in Paris, and is often considered a bridge between Realism and Impressionism.
The National Gallery of Art's East Building in Washington, D.C. was designed by I.M. Pei and opened in 1978.
Bristle brushes are classified by shape: flat, bright, round, filbert, fan, and rigger each serve different purposes.
The Young British Artists (YBAs) movement in the 1990s included Damien Hirst and Tracey Emin and shocked the art world.
Van Gogh created over 2,100 artworks in roughly a decade, including about 860 oil paintings.
Matte medium is mixed with acrylic paint to reduce its glossy finish while maintaining its adhesive properties.
The Broad museum in Los Angeles, designed by Diller Scofidio + Renfro, opened in 2015 with free general admission.
Sgraffito is a technique of scratching through a layer of paint to reveal the layer beneath.
Gamblin Artists Colors, based in Portland, Oregon, is known for producing high-quality, responsibly made oil paints.
The Salon des Refusés in 1863 displayed works rejected by the official Paris Salon, including Manet's scandalous 'Le Déjeuner sur l'herbe.'
Charcoal pencils provide more control than vine charcoal and come in soft, medium, and hard grades.
The Naïve art movement features self-taught artists whose work has a childlike simplicity and directness.
Oil of spike lavender is a natural solvent that was used by painters before turpentine became common.
Katsushika Hokusai created 'The Great Wave off Kanagawa' around 1831 as part of his 'Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji' series.
Installation art transforms an entire space into an immersive experience, engaging multiple senses.
Banksy has confirmed his works only through his website and official Instagram account, never revealing his face.
Venice red, also known as Venetian red, is an earth pigment made from nearly pure iron oxide.
The Doge's Palace in Venice features massive ceiling paintings by Tintoretto and Veronese.
A rigger brush has long, thin bristles and was originally used by ship painters to paint rigging lines.
William Turner was born on April 23, 1775, in London and is considered a precursor to Impressionism.
Photorealism emerged in the late 1960s, with artists creating paintings so detailed they resemble photographs.
Art Deco, popular in the 1920s and 1930s, combined modernist styles with fine craftsmanship and rich materials.
The Serpentine Galleries in London's Hyde Park commission a new temporary pavilion by a famous architect each summer.
Mars black is a synthetic iron oxide pigment that dries faster than ivory black in oil painting.
Édouard Manet's 'Olympia' caused a scandal in 1865 for depicting a nude woman gazing directly at the viewer.
Pen and wash is a drawing technique combining ink linework with watercolor or ink washes for tonal variety.
Frida Kahlo's 'The Two Fridas' (1939) shows two versions of herself, exploring her dual heritage and heartbreak.
Alberto Giacometti's sculpture 'L'Homme au doigt' sold for $141.3 million in 2015 at Christie's.
Winsor & Newton introduced the first moist watercolor pans in 1835, making outdoor painting far more convenient.
Symbolism in the late 19th century rejected realism and naturalism in favor of spiritual and dreamlike imagery.
A stay-wet palette keeps acrylic paints moist for hours or days by using a damp sponge under special paper.
Egon Schiele was born on June 12, 1890, in Tulln an der Donau, Austria.
Aboriginal Australian dot painting is one of the oldest continuous art traditions, spanning over 40,000 years.
Flake white (lead white) produces warm, flexible paint films and was essential for Old Master painters.
The MoMA in New York acquired Van Gogh's 'The Starry Night' in 1941 and it remains one of its most iconic works.
Illusionism in art creates the appearance of reality, from ancient Roman wall paintings to modern trompe-l'oeil.
Schmincke, founded in 1881 in Germany, produces some of the world's finest artist-grade watercolors and oil paints.
René Magritte's 'The Treachery of Images' features a pipe with the text 'Ceci n'est pas une pipe' (This is not a pipe).
Rabbit skin glue has been used for centuries to size canvases before applying gesso and paint.
Light red is a warm, opaque pigment made from calcined yellow ochre and has been used for centuries.
MoMA acquired its first artwork in 1929 — a gift of eight prints and one drawing.
Gouache dries lighter than it appears when wet, which can surprise inexperienced painters.
JMW Turner left over 19,000 sketches and watercolors to the British nation upon his death in 1851.
Caran d'Ache, founded in Geneva in 1915, is known for premium colored pencils and pastels.
International Museum Day is celebrated annually on May 18 to raise awareness of the importance of museums.
Liquin is a popular alkyd medium that speeds up the drying time of oil paints and adds a glossy finish.
The Lascaux cave paintings in France, discovered in 1940, are estimated to be around 17,000 years old.
Tonalism was a late 19th-century American art style emphasizing muted tones and atmospheric effects.
A filbert brush has a flat, oval-shaped tip and is versatile for blending, filling, and detail work.
Cinnabar, a mercury sulfide mineral, was the source of the bright red pigment vermilion used since antiquity.
The Ashmolean Museum in Oxford, founded in 1683, is the world's first university museum.
David Hockney was born on July 9, 1937, in Bradford, England.
Assemblage art creates three-dimensional compositions from found objects, scraps, and everyday materials.
Indian yellow pigment was historically made from the urine of cows fed only mango leaves.
A bone-dry canvas will reject paint — priming with gesso gives the surface 'tooth' for paint to grip.
Jean-Michel Basquiat was born on December 22, 1960, in Brooklyn, New York.
Cloisonnism is a Post-Impressionist style using bold, flat areas of color separated by dark contours.
Gamboge is a bright yellow pigment historically derived from tree resin in Southeast Asia.
The Scala Regia staircase in the Vatican was designed by Bernini using forced perspective to appear longer.
Hot-pressed watercolor paper has a smooth surface ideal for detailed work and fine linework.
Tamara de Lempicka was the leading Art Deco portrait painter of her generation, known for sleek, polished figures.
Caput mortuum is a deep purplish-brown pigment made from iron oxide, popular in Old Master paintings.
The Venice Biennale, established in 1895, is one of the oldest and most prestigious international art exhibitions.
A maquette is a small-scale model or rough draft of an unfinished sculpture or architectural project.
Zorn's palette consists of just four colors — yellow ochre, cadmium red, ivory black, and titanium white.
Neon art, pioneered by artists like Dan Flavin and Bruce Nauman, uses fluorescent and neon light tubes as a medium.
Prussian blue, discovered accidentally in Berlin around 1704, was the first synthetic blue pigment.
Hilma af Klint created abstract paintings starting in 1906, years before Kandinsky, but her work wasn't shown publicly until 1986.
Drypoint engraving scratches lines directly into a metal plate, creating a soft, velvety line quality.
The Dulwich Picture Gallery in London, opened in 1817, was England's first purpose-built public art gallery.
Raw umber pigment comes from natural earth containing iron oxide and manganese oxide.
The Documenta exhibition in Kassel, Germany, is held every five years and is one of the most important contemporary art events.
Auguste Renoir developed severe rheumatoid arthritis but continued painting with brushes strapped to his hands.
Sennelier, a Parisian art supply company founded in 1887, has supplied paints to Picasso, Cézanne, and Monet.
A tonking technique involves pressing newspaper onto a wet oil painting to remove excess paint and reduce texture.
Marcel Duchamp was born on July 28, 1887, near Blainville-Crevon, France.
Naples yellow is one of the oldest synthetic pigments, used since at least 1500 BCE in Babylon.
Outsider art refers to work created by self-taught artists outside the mainstream art world.
The Saatchi Gallery in London helped launch the careers of many Young British Artists in the 1990s.
A medium in painting is any substance mixed with pigment to make paint easier to apply or modify its properties.
